coredata.dc:title: The relationship between diffusion coefficient and ion conductivity in solid-state electrolytes using feedforward neural networks and symbolic regression

coredata.dc:description: Electrolyte materials play a crucial role in enabling ion transport without allowing electron passage, making ionic conductivity and diffusion coefficients key properties for improving technologies such as solid-state batteries. Traditional methods for relating these properties, such as the Nernst–Einstein Equation, fail to apply to solid-state electrolytes due to their assumptions of dilute systems. This study proposes a data-driven approach to bridge this gap, developing two mechanistic models to generate a dataset of 2,000 theoretical materials, focusing on solid-state electrolytes. Feedforward neural networks (FNNs) were employed to predict ionic conductivity from diffusion coefficients, achieving relative error rates below 10% for approximately 95% of cases. Conversely, FNNs also predicted diffusion coefficients from conductivity with 97.45% accuracy, improving to 98.23% when the dissociation reaction was known. Additionally, symbolic regression yielded six mathematical equations, offering a slightly less accurate alternative to the neural networks while substantially easier to implement. Both the neural networks and symbolic equations were validated against experimental data in the literature — highlighting that even though neural networks presented a higher overall accuracy, the symbolic equations presented higher consistency. This work focused on advancing the ability to predict key electrolyte properties using fewer variables, presenting a practical, experimentally feasible solution for the optimization of electrolyte materials in solid-state devices.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrolyte materials play a crucial role in enabling ion transport without allowing electron passage, making ionic conductivity and diffusion coefficients key properties for improving technologies such as solid-state batteries. Traditional methods for relating these properties, such as the Nernst–Einstein Equation, fail to apply to solid-state electrolytes due to their assumptions of dilute systems. This study proposes a data-driven approach to bridge this gap, developing two mechanistic models to generate a dataset of 2,000 theoretical materials, focusing on solid-state electrolytes. Feedforward neural networks (FNNs) were employed to predict ionic conductivity from diffusion coefficients, achieving relative error rates below 10% for approximately 95% of cases. Conversely, FNNs also predicted diffusion coefficients from conductivity with 97.45% accuracy, improving to 98.23% when the dissociation reaction was known. Additionally, symbolic regression yielded six mathematical equations, offering a slightly less accurate alternative to the neural networks while substantially easier to implement. Both the neural networks and symbolic equations were validated against experimental data in the literature — highlighting that even though neural networks presented a higher overall accuracy, the symbolic equations presented higher consistency. This work focused on advancing the ability to predict key electrolyte properties using fewer variables, presenting a practical, experimentally feasible solution for the optimization of electrolyte materials in solid-state devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Solid-state batteries have emerged as a promising frontier in energy storage, offering significant advantages over conventional liquid electrolyte batteries in terms of safety, energy density, and longevity [1]. Their potential to revolutionize applications – from electric vehicles to grid storage – stems largely from the intrinsic benefits of solid electrolytes, which eliminate issues like leakage and flammability common to liquid systems [2]. However, the full promise of solid-state batteries can only be realized by deeply understanding the mechanisms at play within its materials [3,4]. Therefore, to further advance solid-state battery technology, it is essential to optimize key properties of the solid electrolyte core, specially the diffusion coefficient and conductivity [1].

From a fundamental perspective, the very core of an electrolyte material is to allow ion transport across its boundaries without permitting electron transport. Naturally, the ability to efficiently conduct ions is one of the most critical properties of electrolyte materials. As a result, there is ongoing interest in developing new solutions to enhance this characteristic, with the goal of improving the performance and efficiency of technologies like batteries and other electrochemical devices [1].

Numerous modeling studies concentrate on calculating the concentration profile – and ultimately the potential losses – by utilizing the diffusion coefficient of the electrolyte material [5–11]. This coefficient serves as a proportionality constant that quantifies the mass flux of a species in response to a concentration gradient. Conversely, experimental studies often focus on reporting solely the ionic conductivity, σ , usually measured through impedance-based methodologies, representing the mobility of ion species in a media subjected to a given electrical potential [12–14].

Even though these two properties are fundamentally different, both represent the tendency of ionic movement inside a media, and the lack of a clear, concise correlation between both parameters restrains the application of most of the studies that cover only one of them.

Several attempts were made to relate these properties, such as the drift velocity method and the Nernst–Einstein equation [15]. However, such methods are still limited to specific scenarios e.g. the Nernst–Einstein equation is only valid for dilute systems where ions do not interact with each other [16], falling short in case of concentrated electrolytes or electrolytes that contain ion clusters [17], therefore not applying to systems such as solid-electrolytes.

The objective of this work is to develop data-driven models that predict conductivity from diffusion coefficients and vice versa. Given that multiple parameter combinations (e.g., diffusion, concentration, etc.) can yield the same conductivity value, this study focuses on selecting a limited set of input variables that are both accessible and reliable to measure. These inputs are chosen based on the correlation between the input and output for each case, with the goal of maximizing the practical utility of the data-driven models.

To achieve this, we are employing two approaches: first, feedforward neural networks (FNNs), which are trained on existing data to learn complex, non-linear relationships between conductivity and diffusion coefficients, allowing rapid predictions; and second, symbolic regression, which searches for explicit mathematical expressions that best capture the relationship between these variables. We will compare the performance of both approaches to determine the most effective method for accurate and efficient predictions.

In this study, we employed two rigorous first-principle models, previously proposed and validated in the literature, to calculate the conductivity of 2000 theoretical solid-state electrolyte materials with varying diffusion coefficients.

The first model takes into consideration the partial dissociation reaction of the solid-electrolyte, accounting the dynamics of the dissociation reaction along the simulation, while the second simplifies the system into the sole diffusion/migration phenomena, resultant of the total dissociation of the solid-electrolyte before the simulation starts. The reasoning behind the parallel use of two models is that despite the former being theoretically more precise [6,10] the latter is still widely used and proved to be accurate enough to satisfy experimental validations [8,18,19].

For each case (considering or neglecting the partial dissociation reaction), we then optimize the architectures of two FNNs using the HYPERBAND method. These networks are trained on the previously generated data, with one designed to predict conductivity from diffusion coefficients for rapid evaluation of materials, and the other to reverse the process, predicting diffusion coefficients based on desired conductivity values, facilitating efficient material optimization. Additionally, we also employ symbolic regression to find mathematical functions to express the correlation between the two properties.

This work aims to enhance the understanding of the interrelationship between these properties and contribute to the utility of various experimental studies, which have predominantly focused on individual properties, ultimately enabling the solid-state modeling community to leverage such experimental findings documented in the literature - a critical factor in advancing solid-state battery technology.

The ultimate goal of this work is to develop efficient surrogate models, defined by the accuracy of the conversion between diffusion and conductivity, while still relying on parameters that are experimentally accessible to justify the usefulness of our solution.

To accomplish this, two datasets were synthetically generated by using two different electrolyte models: the WR Model, that considers the partial dissociation reaction, and the NR Model, that considers the electrolyte is always fully dissociated. Those mechanistic models will allow the calculation of the ionic conductivity (
Ω
− 1
m
− 1
) based on the model inputs — such as diffusion coefficient and ion concentration at equilibrium. For clarity, the list of inputs for each model is shown in Table 1.

Therefore, by using the latin hypercube sampling (LHS) method, a batch of 1000 input combinations was created to feed each model, resulting in two different datasets that establish the relationship between conductivity and diffusion following the assumptions of two different electrolyte models.
The choice of using two models to work in parallel is based on the fact that while the NR model is widely used in the literature [8,18,19], the WR model is theoretically more precise [6,10].

Then, for each dataset, a confusion matrix was generated in order to understand the impact of every model parameter on the electrolyte conductivity. The inputs with little to no influence were not considered in the development of the data-driven models. Subsequently, two approaches were used to establish the relationship between the conductivity and the remaining model inputs: the FNNs and the symbolic regression.
For clarity, a graphical scheme of the methodology of this work is illustrated in Fig. 1:
Also, in this section, all assumptions considered in each model are clearly outlined, including where, how, and why they were applied. For clarity, these hypothesis are also briefly summarized below:

ce:list-item.ce:para: The model represents a solid-state electrolyte material that dissociates into an ion and counter-ion pair. In the case of the model “NR”, such dissociation already occurred, and no further reaction will be developed. For this reason, no sink–source term is considered. In the case of the model “WR”, such dissociation occurred, and the material starts in the equilibrium state, defined by a constant. However, the (dissociation and association) reaction rates can still be altered according to species concentration following a kinetic law that is further explained. This is the only assumption that differs between model “NR” and model “WR”.
The Nernst–Planck equation describes mass transport and, therefore, it is influenced by diffusion and migration phenomena.

Both species resultant from the material dissociation are under the electroneutrality hypothesis, and therefore present the same concentration (in a given space and time) but still exhibit different physical properties, such as diffusion.

Due to stoichiometric reasons, the initial concentration (
a
0
) of the ion and counter-ion resultant from the electrolyte dissociation will be the same, and their charges (
z
i
) will have the opposite values.
Only the ion can cross the electrolyte surfaces; therefore, both species will present different spacial boundary conditions.
No shunt-currents nor current losses occur in both electrolyte interfaces.
No degradation mechanism is taken into account.

Therefore, by knowing the concentration profile, it is possible to calculate the diffusion and migration overpotential in the same fashion as described in the previous model through the application of Eqs. (7) and (8).

Finally, by employing the two proposed models (NR-Model and WR-Model), it becomes possible to compute the ionic conductivity of a material from any given combination of input parameters, i.e., the material properties listed in Table 1. This capability enables the generation of a synthetic database of materials, which serves as the foundation for training the neural networks and optimizing the symbolic regression algorithms according to the structure showcased in Fig. 1 and that will be further discussed in the next sections.

Data sampling algorithms for the design of experiments (DoE) are essential for training surrogate models because they ensure the efficient exploration of the input space. They capture the system’s variability, prevent overfitting, and enhance the model’s generalization. These algorithms reduce biases and correlations by systematically distributing sample points, resulting in more robust and accurate models.

In this work, the LHS method was chosen to generate input combinations due to its efficiency in exploring the parameter space, ensuring that each variable is sampled uniformly across its entire range [21]. Although alternative methods exist, such as Sobol sampling, which improves parameter space coverage using quasi-random sequences [22], and Monte Carlo sampling, which captures real probabilistic distributions but requires a large number of samples [23], these approaches would not be the most efficient for this study. It has been demonstrated in many applications that LHS is a more efficient sampling method compared to Monte Carlo Sampling (MCS) [24,25]. More advanced methods, such as adaptive sampling based on Gaussian Process Regression (GPR), can reduce the number of required simulations by focusing on regions of higher uncertainty [26]. Still, they come with a significant additional computational cost. Given the objective of this study, which involves generating a large and well-distributed dataset for machine learning models, LHS was the most appropriate choice, as it balances uniform coverage of the parameter space with computational feasibility.

Even though such advantages, some limitations must be considered: (i) LHS assumes a uniform distribution of the input variables, which may not reflect the actual statistical distribution of experimental data [24]; (ii) it assumes independence between variables, which may be inadequate for physicochemical systems where nonlinear correlations between parameters exist [27]; and (iii) it may generate combinations of parameters that are not physically viable, requiring additional constraints to prevent such cases [28].

Nonetheless, through the LHS algorithm, the range of each variable is divided into equal intervals and then one value from each interval is randomly selected, ensuring that samples are spread out evenly [24] - ultimately minimizing unwanted correlations in the input variables, and providing a more comprehensive exploration of the input space than simple random sampling.
By using such technique, it was possible to design 1000 experiments for each electrolyte model between each variable’s minimum and maximum limits, as demonstrated in Table 2.

The respective electrolyte models were used to simulate the concentration profile in each scenario, calculating its overpotential and ultimately estimating the material conductivity over time — making it possible to establish a relationship between the material inputs (represented in Table 2) and outputs (conductivity).

Finally, the dataset obtained from such simulations was randomly divided into three subsets: 70% for training, 15% for validation, and 15% for testing, therefore providing all the data necessary to train the FFNNs and also to develop the equations through the symbolic regression approach.

The first data-driven approach to be implemented is the FNN. For this, the first step is configuring the FNN model, which serves as the non-linear function approximator. This configuration involves estimating several parameters and defining the model’s hyperparameters, which dictate the training and performance of the FNN. Hyperparameters are divided into two sets: model parameters and algorithm parameters. The model parameters include the number of layers, neurons, activation functions, and layer types. The algorithm parameters include the learning rate, momentum, learning rate decay, and the number of epochs for gradient-based optimizers. Proper estimation of these hyperparameters is complex due to their number and varied nature. However, accurately adjusting these hyperparameters is crucial for enhancing the network’s generalization capability and reducing the risk of overfitting.

The relationship between a FNN model’s complexity and its predictive capacity is multifaceted. Complex models can learn intricate patterns but risk overfitting, especially if the data is limited or not varied. Simpler models may only capture some data patterns, leading to underfitting. Balancing model complexity with the quantity and quality of the available data is essential, and regularization techniques are often employed to prevent overfitting.

We employed the HYPERBAND algorithm for hyperparameter optimization to address the trade-off between model complexity and data characteristics [29,30]. It is a technique that improves the efficiency and speed of hyperparameter tuning by employing stochastic sampling and early termination of less efficient configurations by applying the Successive Halving method in an inner loop, saving computational resources by halting less promising trials early.

Applying HYPERBAND involves defining the hyperparameters and their corresponding search spaces. HYPERBAND iteratively adjusts the model’s hyperparameters to maximize performance based on the defined objective function, which is evaluated using training and validation data. This objective function typically includes a loss function to assess model performance. In our study, we focused on the initial learning rate, number of dense layers, activation functions in each layer, and number of neurons in each layer, among other parameters demonstrated in Table 3.

In a symbolic regression problem, a space of mathematical expressions is defined, incorporating building blocks like mathematical operators and state variables. The task of symbolic regression is to search through this space to determine the most effective solution using these basic components. Consequently, the optimization challenge in symbolic regression involves identifying the best structure and parameters for the model [31]. One common objective function in this context is to minimize the mean squared error between the actual values and the values predicted by the model.

In symbolic regression problems, genetic programming (GP) serves as the optimization algorithm, which is a particular form of genetic algorithm (GA). In GP, solutions are depicted as tree-structured chromosomes featuring nodes and branches [31]. For instance, Fig. 2 illustrates a tree-structured chromosome representing the mathematical function
X
0
e
X
1
+ 2
. This tree includes interior nodes for mathematical operations like exp and log and terminal nodes for variables and constants.

The GP algorithm begins by creating trees that consist of randomly generated terminal nodes and mathematical functions varying in size and structure. The user sets the specific mathematical operations and variables used in symbolic regression [32]. The performance of these initial solutions is assessed by comparing their predictions to the actual values using an error metric, such as mean squared error (MSE) [33]. The algorithm then advances the current generation by applying genetic operations such as crossover and mutation. Initially, individuals are selected as parents based on their error values, with those having lower errors being more likely to be chosen. Two individuals are selected during the genetic crossover, and their structures are combined to produce offspring. Specifically, a random subtree from one parent replaces a random subtree in the other parent. This process is illustrated in Fig. 3, where a segment of tree B is incorporated into tree A to create tree C.

In the mutation operation, a single individual is chosen as the parent, and a subtree within this parent is randomly replaced with a new, randomly generated structure. This process is depicted in Fig. 3, where a subtree of tree A is substituted to produce tree D. These mutation operations are performed iteratively until the error value meets predefined criteria or the maximum number of generations is reached [31]. For each generated equation, the consistency of the units of each term is verified to ensure they align with the physical principles of the system.

In this work, we also used the PySRRegressor library from Python [32] to apply symbolic regression for developing mathematical models to describe the conductivity of materials with different diffusion coefficients. For the development of these models, we considered the same training, testing, and validation datasets described previously. The binary operations considered in the symbolic regression were sum, subtraction, multiplication, and division. Moreover, we considered square, cube, exponential, and logarithm in the unary operators.

Considering the range presented in Table 2, two batches of 1000 synthetic experiments each were designed to be run on either the NR (no reaction) Model or in the WR (with reaction) Model. For every case, the concentration profile was determined, and the overpotential was calculated following the methodology presented in the theoretical section of this work. Thus, knowing the overpotential (model output), the external electrical current, and the electrolyte geometry (model inputs), the conductivity was estimated according to Eq. (14):
(14)
σ
( t )
=
L I
η
electrolyte
mt
( t )
A
where σ is the conductivity (
Ω
− 1
m
− 1
), A is the electrolyte area, L is the electrolyte thickness and I is the electrical current.

Notice that, despite several studies determining the conductivity as an intrinsic property of the material, it is, in fact, a dynamic property dependent on the concentration profile of charges inside such material. For example, by observing Eq. (14), it is easy to understand that, in a galvanostatic experiment, the conductivity will vary according to the overpotential presented in the electrolyte (or any other material being examined). As demonstrated in Eq. (8), such overpotential will be ultimately dependent on the concentration profile of the charged species and, therefore, will vary along time following the mass balances presented in the theoretical section of this work.

It is important to note, however, that for all the cases presented in this work, the difference between the average and the maximum/ minimum values of conductivity never had a relative difference higher than 1%. This also explains why most experimental studies find it convenient to consider conductivity as a property intrinsic to the material (regardless of operational variables, such as external current).

As a consequence, in order to relate the diffusion coefficient (fixed value, used as model input) with the conductivity in solid electrolytes (dynamic value, function of time), the surrogate models presented in this work were not designed to be able to describe the dynamic nature of conductivity but to rely on the negligible changes previously mentioned and approximate it as a constant equal to its average value.

Therefore, it was possible to associate all the inputs of the 2000 synthetic experiments simulated with a respective value of conductivity, and the heatmap of correlations (Pearson coefficient) was generated and presented in Fig. 4.

By analyzing Fig. 4, it is possible to note that the correlation between the inputs (all parameters except for conductivity) is not significant, which indicates the input space provided by the LHS was efficiently designed. Thus, it is well suited for this work. Also, it is possible to note the low correlation between the conductivity, electrolyte thickness, and electrical current. This demonstrates that despite the conductivity being a dynamic property dependent on the concentration profile of the electrolyte material, the disturbances in its concentration are insufficient (in the range studied) to affect it significantly. Such fact is in line with the – theoretically wrong, but pragmatically useful – habit of several experimental and modeling studies measuring the conductivity as an intrinsic property of the material and not dependent on the operational conditions (e.g. current) neither extensive properties (e.g. thickness) of the experiment.

This insight leads to the conclusion that not only the conductivity does not significantly vary over time in a given experiment – while the concentration profiles and overpotential are being developed – but also its variation is not significant across different experiments with different values of electrical current and thickness in the range of experiments studied.

Therefore, parameters shown as insignificant in this analysis were not considered meaningful input in both the neural network and symbolic regression approaches. Consequently, despite the NR and WR mechanistic models using such parameters to calculate the conductivity for each case, all the FNNs presented in this study were designed to perform independently of such information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feedforward neural network architecture and nomenclature
ce:section.ce:para: As previously explained, this work approaches the relationship between diffusion and conductivity through the lens of two different assumptions — neglecting or considering the partial dissociation reaction of the solid electrolyte. For the sake of clarity, two families of neural networks were created: the “NR” (as in no reaction) and “WR” (as in with reaction). To estimate conductivity from the diffusion coefficient values and vice versa, two types of neural networks were developed for each family: the “-F” group, representing the “forward strategy” of estimating the conductivity from the diffusion coefficients, and the “-B” group, representing the “backward strategy” of calculating the diffusion coefficients out of the conductivity value.
For the sake of clarity, all four neural networks presented in this work were listed in Table 4, as well as their inputs and outputs.

Notice that, in an idealized scenario, the idea of the “backward” groups being able to estimate all the other parameters (
D
ion
,
D
electron
,
c
0
, δ ) just by knowing the conductivity value ( σ ) is a very attractive concept. However, from a mathematical point of view, this relationship is unlikely to be established since, for every conductivity value, there must be several combinations of parameters in which such conductivity value would be explained. For this reason, to optimize the usefulness of these neural networks, one must select as inputs a small set of variables that, ideally, will be accessible to obtain and reliable to measure while still containing all the information necessary.

This work considered that the accessibility of these parameters is different for each case — for example, obtaining the ion concentration may be less problematic than measuring the ion diffusion coefficient since it is possible to estimate it just by knowing the chemical composition and the material density.

But also, even if the neural network reaches good metrics regarding its loss function, it would not be successful if one of the inputs is unlikely to be known by a user trying to estimate a similar parameter: for example, in case one is trying to obtain the ion diffusion inside a solid electrolyte, it is very likely that the value of electron diffusion in such material is also unknown.

With this in mind, all the “-B” neural networks were designed to estimate both diffusion coefficients (
D
ion
and
D
electron
) using only the inputs shown in Table 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Predicting conductivity from diffusion coefficients: NR-F and WR-F
ce:section.ce:para: During the architecture optimization, the hyperband routine minimized the mean average error (MAE) to a maximum of 200 epochs using the training and validation datasets. The hyperparameters of the best performance are shown in Table 5.

With such architecture, the neural network was trained to minimize the MAE in batch sizes of 64 and maximum epochs of 1000 despite allowing an early stop in case the loss function does not show improvements after 100 consecutive epochs (i.e. patience factor = 100). The development of the loss value across the different epochs is shown in Fig. A.1.

Fig. A.1, shown in the Appendix, illustrates the evolution of loss values during the training and validation processes for two neural network architectures: (a) NR-F and (b) WR-F. The loss values, plotted against the number of epochs, represent the error between the predicted and actual outputs. Both networks exhibit a rapid decrease in loss values during the initial epochs, indicating effective learning and optimization. As training progresses, the loss values stabilize, suggesting that the models are converging toward an optimal solution. The training loss and validation loss follow similar trends in both cases, reflecting balanced generalization, and there is no evidence of overfitting, as the validation loss does not increase significantly compared to the training loss during later epochs. However, the WR-F neural network (b) shows higher fluctuations in validation loss compared to the NR-F neural network (a), implying variability in performance on unseen data. The networks were trained using the Adaptive Moment Estimation (ADAM) algorithm, a widely used optimization technique for neural networks, with Glorot Uniform (Xavier Uniform) initialization ensuring effective parameter distribution at the start of training. This analysis highlights the convergence behavior, absence of overfitting, and generalization performance of both architectures during the training process.

It is possible to notice in Fig. A.1 that in both cases, the convergence criteria were achieved before reaching the maximum epochs value (cf. x-axis values lower than 1000). After training, both neural networks were utilized to predict the conductivity values of 150 samplings - i.e. the test dataset. The inputs were in the range described in Table 2, generated by the LHS Method, and the parity plots comparing the predicted conductivity vs. the conductivity calculated by the mechanistic models are shown in Fig. 5:

By analyzing Fig. 5, it is possible to conclude that both neural networks were effective in surrogating their respective mechanistic models, presenting a relative error lower than 10% for 96% of the cases – for the NR-F Neural Network – and 94% of the cases — for the WR-F Neural Network. It is also possible to observe that the relative deviation increases for lower diffusion coefficient values, where smaller absolute errors have a greater impact on this metric - yielding to the few 5% of cases in which the FFNN presented deviations slightly higher than the 10%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Predicting diffusion coefficients from conductivity: NR-B and WR-B
ce:section.ce:para: As previously discussed, in order to predict conductivity, several material properties are necessary for the mechanistic models presented - i.e. ionic and electronic diffusion coefficients, ion concentration, electrolyte thickness, operating current, and, in case of the dissociation reaction is taken into account, the dissociation fraction in equilibrium and the kinetic constants. In a perfect scenario, it would be possible to run a “reverse-version” of such models and estimate all those parameters just by knowing the measured conductivity. The reality, however, is that such an approach would lead to a degree of freedom problem and therefore there would be several different combinations of such parameters that would be equally possible to explain such value of conductivity if no constraints are imposed. In Fig. 4, it was shown that the electrolyte thickness, operating current, and kinetic constants do not significantly affect the conductivity of a solid-electrolyte. In this section, we propose two neural networks, NR-B and WR-B, that take as input only the conductivity and ion concentration (in case the dissociation reaction is neglected i.e. NR-B), and the dissociation fraction in the equilibrium (in case the dissociation reaction is not neglected i.e. WR-B). In this approach, the architectures of both neural networks were optimized by the hyperband routine, minimizing the MAE to a maximum of 200 epochs per configuration, using the training and validation datasets, in a similar fashion to the procedure shown for the NR-F and WR-F neural networks. The optimized architectures for each neural network are presented in Table 6.

Later, the neural networks were trained in batch sizes of 64 and maximum epochs of 1000, with early stopping applied if the loss function (MAE) showed no improvement over 100 consecutive epochs. The progression of the loss values during the training and validation processes is depicted in Fig. A.2 in Appendix. This figure provides an overview of the learning dynamics for the two architectures, illustrating their optimization behavior across epochs.

As observed, both architectures demonstrate an initial sharp decline in loss values, indicative of effective parameter updates and learning convergence. Over time, the loss values stabilize, confirming the models’ progress toward their respective optimal solutions. Notably, the validation loss trends closely mirror the training loss, which reinforces the models’ generalization capabilities and mitigates concerns regarding overfitting.

It is possible to notice in Fig. A.2 that in both cases, the convergence criteria were achieved before reaching the maximum epochs value (cf. x-axis values lower than 1000). In a similar fashion to the procedure done for the NR-F and WR-F neural networks, the inputs from the test dataset (cf. Table 4) containing 150 samplings were used to compare the predicted ion and electronic diffusion coefficients by the neural networks vs. the values of diffusion coefficients that were used in the mechanistic models. The relative errors are presented in Fig. 6.

By analyzing Fig. 6 it is possible to notice that the relative deviation spikes for lower values of diffusion coefficient, where lower absolute errors will impact more significantly such metric. Nonetheless, the deviations of the predictions of the diffusion coefficients are still substantially higher than the deviations for the conductivity values — as previously approached by NR-F and WR-F. Part of this behavior is caused by the fact that two parameters (
D
ion
and
D
electron
) are being simultaneously estimated, only relying on the conductivity ( σ ), ion concentration (
c
0
), and, in case the dissociation reaction is not being neglected, the dissociation fraction in equilibrium ( δ ).

Also, it is important to note that, differently from the conductivity, the diffusion coefficient is way less sensitive in terms of determining the battery concentration profile and overpotential, sometimes demanding variations in orders of magnitude to translate into meaningful variations in voltaic efficiency [11]. As a consequence, even experimental studies often diverge among themselves while estimating such properties. As an example, the Li
7
La
3
Zr
2
O12 (LLZO) has its bulk room temperature diffusion coefficient reported in experimental studies as 2–8 × 10−13 [34], 2.15 × 10−9 [35], and 0.64 × 10−15 in molecular dynamic studies [36]. In this context, the neural networks proposed in this study are still able to simultaneously predict the correct order of magnitude of both the ionic and electronic diffusion coefficients in 97.96% and 96.94% of the cases, respectively, even though no information regarding the dissociation reaction is provided (NR-B). In case the fraction of dissociation in equilibrium ( δ ) is known, these values increase to 97.98% and 98.48% of the cases, for the estimation of the ionic and electronic diffusion, respectively, by using the WR-B neural network.

Using the same training and validation datasets as the neural networks (generated from the LHS method inside the limits described in Table 2), the symbolic regression routine was described in the theoretical section of this work. Following this approach, three equations were generated as candidates to express the relationship between diffusion and conductivity constants (without considering the dissociation reaction) in a similar fashion to the training procedure of the NR-F neural network. Thus, three other equations were generated to express the diffusion–conductivity relationship, considering the dissociation reaction analogously to the training of the WR-F neural network. Notice that, here, we also kept the same reasoning behind the choice of inputs/outputs of these functions presented in Table 4. For clarity, all these six equations were labeled and explicitly showed in Table 7, below.

After the convergence of the six equations proposed, all of them were used to predict the conductivity value of a test dataset that was not provided during the training phase — the same as used in the test phase of the neural networks presented previously (NR-F and WR-F). The results are represented in Fig. 7.

By analyzing Fig. 7, it is possible to see that the simpler case, Equation NR-#1, is not able to predict the conductivity values of the test dataset satisfactorily. For Equations with higher complexity, the prediction improvement is noticeable, and the equations become even more precise when we take the dynamic aspect of the dissociation reaction, as it is possible to observe in the cases of “WR” group of equations.

Therefore, the symbolic equations proposed in Table 7 achieved a satisfactory approximation within the range of values tested, from 0 to ca. 55
( Ω m )
− 1
.

Furthermore, it is important to highlight the significant reduction in parameter count when comparing the neural network approaches with the equations presented here. For example, while the neural network models range from 157,761 parameters (NR-F) to 767,138 parameters (WR-B), a symbolic regression model such as Equation WR-#3 may require as few as 4 parameters and still deliver outstanding performance.

This drastic difference is relevant because the computational expense of a model is closely tied to its number of parameters. Neural networks – with hundreds of thousands of parameters in our case – require extensive matrix multiplications, non-linear activations, and iterative gradient-based optimizations during training. This complexity translates into significant memory usage and longer inference times, especially when deploying on hardware with limited resources. In contrast, a symbolic equation with only four parameters involves just a few arithmetic operations, resulting in minimal computational overhead and nearly instantaneous evaluation.

Thus, while neural networks provide flexibility in capturing intricate, non-linear relationships, the symbolic approach is markedly more efficient in terms of computational expense and contributing to the explanation of why they could represent a viable approach even in instances where some performance metric, such as the mean square error (MSE), may be slightly inferior compared to neural networks - a notion that will be examined in the further sections.

As explained previously, when starting from the conductivity to predict diffusion parameters, it may be useful to predict both diffusion coefficients simultaneously for convenience — even though this attempt may lead to a problem of degrees of freedom. This situation is even more explicit in the symbolic solution, where we cannot simultaneously estimate two variables (
D
ion
and
D
electron
) with a single equation. To circumvent this problem, and maintain the same convenience of the neural-network-based solutions, we propose to combine two of the equations listed in Table 7 and solve them as a system to estimate both diffusion parameters just by knowing the conductivity ( σ ), ion concentration (
c
0
) and when the dissociation reaction is not being neglected, the dissociation fraction at equilibrium ( δ ).

In this regard, we merged the two equations with higher complexity (i.e. “-#2” case and “-#3” case) and numerically solved the system to find both the ionic and electric diffusion coefficient neglecting the dynamic aspect of the dissociation reaction (“NR-” group) and considering it (“WR-” group). The results are presented in Fig. 8.

By analyzing Fig. 8, it is possible to conclude that by combining two equations, in each case, it was possible to reach a solution that allows to estimate, satisfactorily, both diffusion coefficients simultaneously in a similar fashion to the results presented when the neural networks performed the task. As a reference, the results presented in Fig. 8 show that these systems of equations were able to correctly predict the order of magnitude of both the ionic and electronic diffusion coefficients in 96.4% and 97.9% of the cases, respectively, even though no information regarding the dissociation reaction is provided. Furthermore, these values change to 91.4% and 93.4% of the cases, for the estimation of the ionic and electronic diffusion, respectively, if replacing the model that considers the partial dissociation reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison and validation: Neural networks vs. symbolic approach
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, rather than emphasizing the development process and absolute performance of each individual model, we focus on evaluating their relative effectiveness through a series of comparative analyses. The objective of this section is to assess not only the efficacy of each model against one another but also their potential for future validation against experimental data reported in the literature. Ultimately, the models’ performance is considered in relation to the Nernst–Einstein Equation, which — despite being originally formulated for liquid, weak electrolytes [16,17] - remains a widely adopted reference in the scientific community for this type of conversion.

The first analysis involves quantifying the mean squared error (MSE) between the conductivity values predicted by the proposed models and those generated by the mechanistic model, which served as the source of the training, testing, and validation datasets used in this study. The results of this comparison are presented in Table 8.

As shown in Table 8, and in line with expectations, models characterized by higher structural complexity achieved lower mean squared error (MSE) values. Additionally, the “WR-” models, which account for partial dissociation reactions, demonstrated improved fitting performance when compared to the “NR-” models that neglect this effect.

Next, to further underscore the relevance and applicability of the approaches proposed in this work, a brief literature review was conducted to collect experimental values for diffusion coefficients and ionic conductivities reported in independent studies. Priority was given to materials relevant to solid-state battery research. One additional material,
Li
7
P
3
S
11
, was also included in the analysis due to its diffusion coefficient falling outside the training range specified in Table 2. This inclusion offers preliminary insight into the models’ extrapolation capabilities. The list of selected materials, along with their reported diffusion coefficients, densities, and corresponding references, is presented in Table 9.

As shown in Table 10, both the neural network models and the equations derived from symbolic regression were able to predict the conductivity of the four studied materials within the correct order of magnitude. For these materials — whose diffusion coefficients fall within the training range defined in Table 2 - the neural network model WR-F yielded predictions most closely aligned with those of the symbolic equations (WR-#1 and WR-#3), consistent with the trends observed earlier in Table 8.

In contrast, for the fifth material, whose diffusion coefficients lie outside the training range, the WR-F neural network significantly overestimated the conductivity, predicting a value approximately 100 times higher than the experimental measurement. Remarkably, both symbolic equations maintained a relative error of approximately 17%, underscoring their superior performance in extrapolation tasks and highlighting the limitation of neural networks in such scenarios.

The subsequent analysis focused on the inverse scenario: the estimation of both diffusion parameters using pairs of predictive equations, rather than using diffusion data to estimate conductivity. In this context, the performance trend observed in previous sections does not consistently hold. Specifically, the “WR-” models do not necessarily provide more accurate predictions compared to their “NR-” counterparts. This shift in performance is illustrated in Table 11.

Overall, the results indicate that the models captured the underlying relationships between input parameters and target properties, reinforcing their applicability for predictive analysis in this context. Nonetheless, minor deviations may still occur due to factors such as experimental noise, slight inconsistencies in data preprocessing, and inherent approximations in the modeling process. These deviations can be attributed to limitations in capturing highly nonlinear interactions, minor variations in feature importance across different parameter ranges, or residual systematic errors in the dataset.

To mitigate these errors, the dataset was constructed to ensure broad parameter coverage and preprocessing techniques such as outlier detection and feature scaling were applied. Additionally, hyperparameter tuning and statistical validation were performed to optimize model accuracy and consistency.

Furthermore, while the models demonstrated strong performance within the observed data range, their predictive accuracy may decrease in extreme conductivity regimes, where very low or very high values introduce challenges in model generalization. Neural networks, in particular, may struggle with extrapolation beyond the training data range, potentially leading to increased errors when predicting out-of-distribution values. Abrupt transitions in system behavior or highly nonlinear dependencies could further impact the precision of predictions. Symbolic regression models, despite their interpretability, also face challenges related to balancing complexity and generalization, as overly complex models may overfit specific data regions, particularly in cases of data sparsity. These factors highlight the need to carefully consider data preprocessing, model selection, and validation strategies to ensure reliable predictions across various conditions.

Finally, although the Nernst–Einstein Equation is, theoretically, unsuitable for describing the relationship between diffusion and conductivity in solid electrolytes [16,17], it was still included in the analysis for comparative purposes. Specifically, the validation dataset — comprising 200 samples of fictitious materials with properties within the range defined in Table 2 and excluded from the training process — was evaluated using the Nernst–Einstein Equation and compared against the models proposed in this study. As in previous evaluations, the primary criteria was the ability to correctly predict the order of magnitude of the material conductivity. The results of this comparison are summarized in Table 12.

Contrary to what might be expected based on prior results, the WR-F neural network did not achieve the highest overall score in this final evaluation — despite maintaining the lowest mean squared error (MSE) among all models. This outcome suggests that its strong average performance may obscure a smaller subset of cases in which the model produces unacceptably large errors, revealing a limitation in robustness when applied to edge cases.

In contrast, the symbolic equations achieved the highest scores in this evaluation, underscoring their consistency and reliability. Although they exhibit a higher MSE compared to the neural networks, their stability across both several scenarios makes them particularly compelling. The Nernst–Einstein Equation, as expected, yielded the lowest overall performance. Nevertheless, this classical approach — originally developed for weak, liquid electrolytes [16,17] - was still able to predict the correct order of magnitude in approximately 60% of the cases. This level of accuracy helps explain its continued widespread use across various electrolyte systems in the literature.

Finally, it is important to emphasize that, despite their slightly higher MSE, the symbolic regression models presented in this study produced robust and consistent estimates for both synthetic and real materials. Notably, for some cases they also demonstrated the ability to extrapolate beyond the training domain, successfully predicting the order of magnitude for experimental cases outside the specified range. These advantages, coupled with their interpretability and ease of implementation compared to more complex machine learning frameworks, highlight the practical value of symbolic models in this context.

In this paper, data-driven approaches were employed to establish the relationship between diffusion coefficients and conductivity. This work not only systematically reduced the need for multiple variables typically essential in electrolyte models but also took into consideration the experimental feasibility of measuring inputs for each surrogate model aiming for a solution that is both mathematically consistent and pragmatically useful. At the same time, we maintained a relative error in conductivity estimation below 10% in most cases and accurately predicted the order of magnitude for both diffusion coefficients, despite their lower sensitivity compared to conductivity i.e. diffusion coefficients ranged from
1
0
− 17
m
2
s
− 1
to
1
0
− 12
m
2
s
− 1
, while conductivity varied between 0.01 and
60
Ω
− 1
m
− 1
. Moreover, we identified symbolic equations that, although slightly less accurate than FNNs, offer sufficient precision and are easier to implement.

Both the diffusion coefficients of charged species inside electrolyte materials and the conductivity of such materials, despite being different properties, strongly contribute to and represent how easily a given charge can travel across the media. Previous efforts in the literature attempted to relate them with methods such as the drift velocity method and the Nernst–Einstein Equation — which are only valid for specific scenarios that exclude solid-state electrolytes.

In this work, we employed two mechanistic models to generate a dataset by simulating and calculating the conductivity of 2000 theoretical materials. Based on these data, two FNNs were developed to represent the link between material properties (such as the ionic and electronic diffusion, ion concentration, and the dissociation fraction at equilibrium if such reaction is not neglected) and the respective conductivity value.

The results showed that the neural networks could predict the conductivity with a relative error lower than 10% for ca. 95% of cases, even when no information regarding the dissociation reaction is known. Furthermore, two other neural networks were developed based on the same dataset, simultaneously predicting both the ionic and electronic diffusion coefficient out of conductivity information and correctly guessing the order of magnitude of such parameters in an average of 97.45% of the cases – with no information regarding the dissociation reaction – and increasing to 98.23% in case the fraction of dissociation in equilibrium is known.

Also, a symbolic regression was implemented with the same data used to train the neural networks, and six equations were proposed to represent such a relationship — three considering the properties related to the dissociation reaction and three neglecting them. Such an approach allowed the prediction of both diffusion coefficients in the same order of magnitude for 97.19% of the cases (with no reaction considered).

Lastly, the effectiveness of each model was evaluated using experimental data from the literature. While the WR-F neural network achieved the slightly better predictions, the symbolic equations WR-#1 and WR-#3 were also able to accurately predict the conductivity of a material outside the training range, maintaining an impressive deviation of about 17% even under these extrapolative conditions.

Overall, this work successfully demonstrated that data-driven approaches are able to significantly reduce the number of variables typically required in electrolyte models. The approach also considered the experimental feasibility of measuring inputs, providing a solution that is both mathematically consistent and practically useful. Besides, symbolic equations were identified, offering adequate precision and easier implementation, albeit with slightly less accuracy than FNNs. Nonetheless, exploring how the material/crystal geometry impact such properties and the influence of significant temperature variations on this relationship remains an important avenue for future investigation.

ce:section.ce:para: Figs. A.1 and A.2 shows the evolution of loss values during the training and validation processes for two neural network architectures, NR-F and WR-F. These graphs were generated as part of the performance evaluation process of the architectures, highlighting the learning dynamics and generalization capacity of each model.
